content:
11 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Galina Nelyubova on UnsplashGeneral Adoption
Every major technology wave begins with a promise. The steam engine promised scale. Electricity promised productivity. Computers promised accuracy. The internet promised access. Cloud computing promised flexibility. Artificial intelligence now promises something more intimate, intelligence on demand. It tells organisations that writing, coding, analysis, coaching, research, service, and decision support can happen faster than before.
In my opinion, the danger is not that companies are adopting AI. They should. The danger is that many companies are adopting AI during a period of financial pressure, investor impatience, and fear of being left behind. According to Computerworld, the technology sector’s 2023 layoffs came after a pandemic-era hiring surge, when digital demand rose sharply and later normalised. Computerworld cited Layoffs.fyi data showing about 262,682 tech layoffs across 1,186 companies in 2023, after 164,969 layoffs in 2022. That matters because AI did not arrive in a calm labour market. It arrived inside a workplace already being resized.
There are two fires burning inside organisations today. The first is the visible belief that AI will replace people and that smaller teams automatically mean better management. The second is quieter but equally dangerous, the rising cost of token usage. Every prompt, file upload, generated summary, agent workflow, model call, and repeated attempt consumes tokens, compute, credits, and money. Many companies govern headcount with discipline. In my view, they are not yet governing AI consumption with the same seriousness.
Concept of Efficiency
For more than a century, organisations have tried to make work measurable. Scientific management broke labour into tasks. Assembly lines standardised movement. Enterprise software standardised processes. Outsourcing moved work to lower-cost locations. Shared services centralised administration. Cloud computing turned infrastructure into consumption. Each wave carried the same business logic. When work can be measured, it can be optimised. When it can be optimised, waste can be reduced. When waste falls, margins improve.
I believe the logic is not wrong. Businesses must be efficient to survive. But somewhere along the way, efficiency became emotionally neutral. A company could remove layers and call it simplification. It could outsource work and call it globalisation. It could close offices and call it footprint optimisation. Today, it can reduce people and call it AI transformation. This is where I feel leaders must be more honest. A layoff may be financially necessary. A restructure may be strategically justified. A role may genuinely change because of technology. But when AI becomes the blanket explanation, the human story disappears.
Harvard Business Review has warned that companies may be laying off workers because of AI’s potential, not necessarily because of proven current capability, and that this can create cynicism, damage trust, and force companies into embarrassing rehiring later. That distinction matters. Cutting because a process has truly been redesigned is one thing. Cutting because a future tool might one day absorb the work is another.
Imminent Cuts
Microsoft, LinkedIn, and Google matter in this conversation because they shape corporate language. What Big Tech does often becomes the vocabulary of everyone else. Smaller companies watch how these firms explain restructuring, how investors respond, and how quickly the public conversation moves on. Over time, difficult workforce decisions become normalised. The language softens before the pain does.
According to Reuters reporting published by Inc., Microsoft said in July 2025 that it would cut nearly 4 percent of its workforce, around 9,000 workers, after a previous round affecting around 6,000 employees in May. The same report connected the cuts to Microsoft’s effort to rein in costs amid heavy AI infrastructure investment. LinkedIn, owned by Microsoft, announced 668 cuts in October 2023, according to TechCrunch, bringing its total cuts that year to 1,384. Google also announced about 12,000 role reductions in January 2023 through a message from Sundar Pichai, who said the company had hired for a different economic reality and needed to focus resources on priorities including AI.
My concern is not that these companies made cuts. Large organisations will always restructure. My concern is that AI is becoming the language that makes cuts sound inevitable, intelligent, and almost progressive. The market may hear discipline. Employees may hear something colder. They may hear that they must become part of the AI story or risk being written out of it.
Replacement Story
There is a difference between using AI to redesign work and using AI as a convenient explanation for decisions leaders already wanted to make. AI removes some tasks, changes many roles, creates new capabilities, and exposes old inefficiencies. But it does not automatically tell leaders what kind of organisation they should build. That responsibility remains human.
According to Challenger, Gray and Christmas, AI was the leading reason cited for U.S. job cuts in June 2026 for the fourth consecutive month. AI was cited in 14,029 cuts in June and 101,743 cuts through the first half of 2026. Yet the broader labour story is not only about disappearance. The World Economic Forum’s Future of Jobs Report 2025 projects 170 million new roles and 92 million displaced roles by 2030, resulting in a net increase of 78 million jobs. The Economist has also framed AI as more likely to reshape white-collar work than wipe it out, drawing on the longer history of technological revolutions.
My thought is this, when we step back from the headlines and look at the work itself, AI is not just a replacement story. It is a redesign story. If leaders treat it only as a replacement tool, they will cut faster than they rebuild. They will remove people before understanding the work those people actually hold. They will underestimate tacit knowledge, customer memory, cultural context, and judgment. A spreadsheet can show salary savings. It cannot show the cost of losing someone who remembers why the previous transformation failed.
Economist Daron Acemoglu has warned that AI may raise average productivity while also replacing workers or degrading job quality for those who remain. His argument is important because productivity does not automatically become shared prosperity. In my opinion, this is the point many leaders avoid. The question is not only whether AI makes work faster. The question is who benefits, who carries the risk, and whether the people who helped build the company are given a serious path into its next version.
Trust Placement
When leaders speak about AI transformation, they often use the language of systems. They talk about platforms, agents, operating models, productivity, workflow automation, and cost takeout. Employees hear a different language. They hear uncertainty. They hear relevance. They hear survival. They hear the silent question of whether they will still matter next year.
Simon Sinek’s writing on trust is especially relevant here. According to Sinek, organisations undermine trust through actions such as mass layoffs, false promises, and excessive control without autonomy. In my view, AI adoption will fail in many companies not because the tools are weak, but because the emotional foundation is weak. Employees will not experiment openly with a technology they believe is being used to quietly measure, rank, or replace them.
Harvard Business Review makes a similar point through the lens of psychological safety. It argues that AI integration often changes how teams work together, and that the real impact can show up as declining interpersonal trust, weaker coordination, and poorer decision-making. The recommendation is not to treat AI adoption only as a tech upgrade, but as a team development effort. That is the leadership work. It is slower than buying software, but without it, adoption becomes compliance rather than belief.
This matters because people do not learn well when they feel hunted. They protect themselves. They hide mistakes. They stop asking basic questions. They nod in meetings and disengage after. A company may see dashboards showing AI usage, but beneath the dashboard, employees may be wondering whether every efficiency they create will become evidence against their own job.
Valuable Tokens
The second fire is less emotional but just as serious. Most employees experience AI as a simple chat box. They type, receive an answer, and move on. It feels light. It feels almost free. But underneath that experience sits a metered economic model. Every prompt, uploaded file, long conversation, generated report, image, audio file, and agent action consumes tokens.
According to Deloitte, tokens are becoming the fundamental unit of AI economics, and leaders should govern AI as an economic system rather than manage it with outdated cost models. Deloitte also says organisations should use real-time monitoring, budget alerts, chargebacks, right-sized models, and FinOps practices to prevent runaway costs. The message is clear. AI spend is not a normal software licence. It behaves more like a living consumption engine.
The FinOps Foundation explains why this can become expensive in practice. It warns that real generative AI cost is shaped by operational details such as model choice, input and output differences, multimodal content, and context window growth. In long conversations, prior context may be repeatedly resent so the model can maintain continuity. What looks like one chat to a user can become a growing trail of token consumption to the company.
My concern is that many organisations apply strict discipline to people cost while allowing AI usage to grow with far less scrutiny. A manager may need multiple approvals to hire one analyst. The same team may run thousands of prompts, generate multiple versions of reports, activate agents, and consume model capacity without anyone asking whether the output created measurable value. That is not innovation. That is unmanaged consumption wearing the clothes of transformation.
Scale
Google’s own AI usage shows where enterprise AI is heading. At Google I/O 2026, Sundar Pichai said Google’s model APIs were processing about 19 billion tokens per minute and that more than 375 Google Cloud customers had each processed more than one trillion tokens in the previous 12 months. Most companies will never operate at Google’s scale, but the direction matters. AI is moving from a tool people open into a layer embedded across email, documents, CRM, HR systems, finance platforms, coding tools, customer service, analytics, and knowledge management.
Once AI becomes embedded everywhere, the question cannot simply be whether employees are using it. The better question is whether the usage is creating value. Microsoft’s own documentation for certain Microsoft 365 Copilot experiences describes cost dashboards, budgets, alerts, spending policies, prepaid credits, pay-as-you-go billing, and hard caps for usage-based billing through Copilot Credits. That is responsible design, but it also proves the larger point. If AI can be metered, it can run away. If it can run away, it must be governed.
Economist Enterprise has also highlighted the physical cost beneath AI, especially in data centres where cooling, power density, water use, and specialised infrastructure become critical as AI workloads rise. This matters because token usage is not just a line item in a software dashboard. It connects to compute, power, cooling, capital expenditure, and environmental pressure. The prompt may feel weightless, but the infrastructure behind it is not.
